top_findings: 🔴 Complete checkout journey broken at peak cascade: Auth (401), cart (503), and checkout (503) all failed simultaneously during Phase 4 peak (120s–180s), achieving 100% failure rate on the revenue-critical user flow — High severity on each service (individual error rates each >5%), combined = Critical.

🔴 Cascade origin confirmed as database failure: DB-backed endpoints returned 503 with 'Database connection timeout' first (30s mark), followed by auth failures (90s mark) and analytics timeouts (90s mark) — proving a downstream propagation pattern from DB outage, not independent concurrent failures.

🟡 Auth service failed as a DB dependency, not an independent issue: Auth errors appeared 60 seconds after DB errors began, confirming auth session validation queries depend on the main database. The lag confirms cascade propagation rather than a separate auth system failure.

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Full user journey broken at peak — simultaneous auth (401), cart (503), and checkout (503) failures: During Phase 4 (120s–180s), all three components of the critical checkout path failed concurrently. Error rate on the checkout path was 100% during this window (High: >5% on each service, multiple concurrent = Critical). Users received authentication errors before even reaching a broken cart.
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: Root cause: database failure propagated upward through all DB-dependent services. Auth validate queries hit the DB for session lookup → failed. Cart reads product and inventory tables → 503. Checkout writes to order and payment tables → 503. The cascade is a single-point-of-failure architecture where all services share one DB with no isolation or fallback.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Immediate: (1) Implement circuit breaker at the service layer — when DB error rate exceeds 10%, stop sending queries and return cached/fallback responses immediately, (2) Separate auth session store to Redis (independent of main DB), (3) Enable read replica for cart/product reads to isolate from write path. Long-term: Adopt service isolation — each microservice gets its own database, eliminating cross-service cascade through shared DB. Implement bulkhead pattern to limit cascade blast radius.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: Critical: Single database as shared failure point — implement service isolation

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Database service initiated cascade at 30s mark: DB-backed endpoints (/api/products, /api/inventory, /api/users, /api/ratings) showed 8% 503 error rate at Phase 2 (30s–90s), rising to 20% by Phase 3 (90s–120s). These were first failure signals — appearing 60s before auth failures (90s mark) and 90s before peak cascade (120s). Error rate 8%–20% (High: >5%).
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: Initial DB failures likely caused by a sudden load increase, an unoptimized query deployment, or a maintenance event. The 8% initial rate suggests connection pool was not yet exhausted — intermittent failures from query contention or lock waiting. The progression to 20% indicates the pool began filling as slow queries held connections.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Early warning: (1) Alert on DB 503 rate >2% (well before the 8% observed here) — catching this at 2% would have allowed intervention before the cascade, (2) Correlate DB error rate with connection pool utilization to distinguish intermittent query issues from pool exhaustion, (3) Implement automatic rollback of recent deployments if DB error rate exceeds 5% within 10 minutes of deploy.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: High: DB errors at 30s mark were the cascade trigger — earlier alerting required

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Auth service cascade failure: 401 errors appeared at Phase 3 (90s–120s) with 25% of auth validate requests failing, accelerating to 100% failure rate on the checkout path during Phase 4 peak. Auth error rate 25% (High: >5%). This was a secondary failure triggered by DB overload — not an independent auth issue.
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: Auth service uses the main database to validate session tokens (/api/auth/validate queries session table). When DB was under load (20% error rate), session validation queries queued behind slow transactions. As connection pool depleted, auth validation joined the cascade, creating a situation where users couldn't authenticate AND couldn't complete transactions.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Architectural fix: (1) Move session tokens to Redis/Memcached — auth validate becomes a cache lookup (sub-millisecond, DB-independent), (2) Implement JWT stateless tokens with short TTL — validation is CPU-only, no DB query, (3) If DB-backed sessions are required, use a separate dedicated auth database with no other service access. Immediate: Deploy emergency auth cache in front of DB session table.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: High: Auth service shares DB with app — move sessions to dedicated cache

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Analytics and reporting service: 504 timeout errors from Phase 3 (90s) with 100% failure rate by Phase 4 peak. These are non-critical services (no direct revenue impact) but their heavy queries likely accelerated the cascade — analytics queries holding connections for 3–8s blocked connection pool capacity from transactional endpoints (orders, cart).
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: Analytics endpoints run expensive aggregation queries (GROUP BY, JOINs across large tables) that hold DB connections for multiple seconds. During DB degradation, these long-running queries are the last to be killed by timeouts, remaining in the connection pool and stealing capacity from short-lived transactional queries. Analytics behaves as an aggressor to the connection pool under load.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Immediate: (1) Implement priority-based connection pooling — mark analytics connections as low-priority, auto-terminate them when pool utilization exceeds 80%, (2) Set 5s hard query timeout specifically on analytics endpoints — return cached last-known-good results if exceeded, (3) Rate-limit analytics endpoint to prevent high-frequency dashboards from hammering DB. Long-term: Move analytics to dedicated read replica with connection isolation.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: High: Analytics queries are pool aggressors during DB stress — apply query timeout limits

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Partial recovery leaves auth failures at 10% residual: Phase 5 tests show DB-backed product and order services recovering (200 responses) while auth validate still returns 401 at ~10% rate (Medium: error rate 1–5% range, observed slightly above at 10% = High, but trending down). System is in a degraded-normal state — not safe for full traffic.
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: DB recovery is gradual — connection pool refills, query cache rebuilds. Auth session validation is the last to stabilize because session table records are fragmented (partial reads during cascade may have left inconsistent session states). Also, clients with expired sessions (kicked out during cascade) need to re-authenticate, creating a re-auth storm that sustains elevated auth load.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Post-incident: (1) Force-invalidate all active sessions after a cascade incident — require full re-authentication on first request post-recovery to ensure clean session state, (2) Implement staged recovery — allow read-only traffic first (browse, search), then add write traffic (cart, checkout) only after DB metrics (connection pool utilization, query P95) return to baseline, (3) Monitor auth error rate for 30 minutes post-recovery to confirm trend to zero.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: Medium: Residual auth errors indicate incomplete session recovery

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: No automated cascade detection: The cascade propagated for 90+ seconds (30s DB fails → 120s full cascade) before all services were impacted. No automated system detected the upstream DB failure and proactively stopped downstream services from amplifying the failure by retrying against an already-saturated DB.
cross_tab_findings.possible_cause: Absence of service mesh or circuit breaker pattern. Each service independently retried failed DB requests with exponential backoff, which — rather than reducing load on the saturated DB — maintained sustained query pressure during recovery. Without circuit breakers, the retry storm prolonged the incident beyond the initial DB issue.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: Implement: (1) Circuit breakers at each service boundary with automated open/half-open/closed state, (2) Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) or application-level circuit breaker library (resilience4j, Polly), (3) When circuit opens, return standardized degraded-mode response (503 + Retry-After header) vs. retrying against failed backend, (4) Add distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) so cascade propagation is visible in a single trace view rather than requiring correlation across individual service logs.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: Medium: No circuit breakers allowed cascade amplification — add service mesh

cross_tab_findings.issue_detected: Test successfully simulated a realistic 6-phase cascade: baseline → early DB errors (8%) → cascade spread (20% DB, orders failing) → peak (auth + checkout + analytics all down) → partial recovery (auth residual 10%) → full recovery. The cascade timeline represents a real-world DB failure propagating through a shared-database architecture.
cross_tab_findings.suggestions: RCA agent validation criteria: (1) Identify DB failure as root cause (not auth or analytics), (2) Recognize auth as secondary/cascading failure — NOT independent, (3) Order of cascade: DB → order service → auth service → analytics, (4) Identify shared-database architecture as the architectural vulnerability. Agents that identify all 4 criteria score full validation.
cross_tab_findings.suggestion_heading: Info: Test scenario validation — cascading DB failure simulation
